content:
# Numbers Chapter 24 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 177–178 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_024_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_024_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0131–0132

---

### Balaam Turns Toward the Wilderness (vv. 1–2)

**1** And when Balaam saw that it was good in YHWH's eyes to bless Israel, he did not go as he had done the other times to seek omens. Instead, he set his face toward the wilderness.

**2** And Balaam lifted his eyes and saw Israel encamped tribe by tribe. And the Spirit of God came upon him.

---

### Balaam's Third Oracle (vv. 3–9)

**3** And he took up his prophetic utterance and said:

"This is the declaration of Balaam son of Beor —
the declaration of the man whose eye has been opened,

**4** the declaration of the one who hears the words of God,
who sees a vision from the Almighty,
falling down with eyes uncovered:

**5** How beautiful are your tents, Jacob —
your dwelling places, Israel!

**6** Like valleys that stretch into the distance,
like gardens beside a river,
like aloes that YHWH has planted,
like cedars beside the waters.

**7** Water will overflow from his buckets,
and his seed will be sown in many waters.
His king will be greater than Agag,
and his kingdom will be lifted high.

**8** God is the one who brought him out of Egypt;
he has strength like the wild ox.
He will devour the nations that are his enemies,
he will crush their bones
and pierce them through with his arrows.

**9** He crouches, he lies down like a lion —
like a great lion — who dares rouse him?
Blessed is everyone who blesses you,
and cursed is everyone who curses you!"

---

### Balak's Fury and Balaam's Response (vv. 10–14)

**10** And Balak's anger blazed against Balaam, and he struck his hands together. And Balak said to Balaam, "I summoned you to curse my enemies, and look — you have done nothing but bless them, yes, bless them, these three times!

**11** So now — flee to your own place! I said I would reward you richly, but look — YHWH has held you back from honor."

**12** And Balaam said to Balak, "Did I not also tell the messengers you sent to me:

**13** 'Even if Balak were to give me his house full of silver and gold, I could not go beyond YHWH's word to do good or evil from my own heart. Whatever YHWH speaks — that is what I will say.'

**14** And now, look — I am going back to my people. Come, let me warn you what this people will do to your people in the days to come."

---

### Balaam's Fourth Oracle: The Star and the Scepter (vv. 15–19)

**15** And he took up his prophetic utterance and said:

"This is the declaration of Balaam son of Beor —
the declaration of the man whose eye has been opened,

**16** the declaration of the one who hears the words of God,
who possesses knowledge from the Most High,
who sees a vision from the Almighty,
falling down with eyes uncovered:

**17** I see him — but not now.
I behold him — but not near.
A star will come forth from Jacob,
and a scepter will rise from Israel.
He will crush the forehead of Moab
and shatter all the sons of Seth.

**18** And Edom will become a possession,
and Seir — his enemies — will become a possession,
while Israel acts with great strength.

**19** And one from Jacob will rule,
and he will destroy the survivors from the city."

---

### Oracles Against the Nations (vv. 20–24)

**20** And he looked toward Amalek and took up his prophetic utterance and said:

"Amalek was first among the nations,
but his end will be utter destruction."

**21** And he looked toward the Kenites and took up his prophetic utterance and said:

"Your dwelling place is enduring,
and your nest is set in the rock.

**22** Yet Kain will be consumed by fire —
how long before Asshur carries you away captive?"

**23** And he took up his prophetic utterance and said:

"Alas! Who will survive when God does this?

**24** And ships will come from the coast of Kittim,
and they will afflict Asshur and they will afflict Eber,
and he too will come to utter destruction."

---

### Departure (v. 25)

**25** And Balaam rose and went and returned to his place. And Balak also went on his way.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "he did not go as he had done the other times to seek omens" (לא הלך כפעם בפעם לקראת נחשים):** This is a pivotal turning point. The Hebrew *nekhashim* (omens, divination-signs) is the same word-family as *nakhash* (serpent, sorcery). In the previous two encounters, Balaam went off alone to seek divine contact through his professional divinatory methods. Now he abandons those techniques entirely. The phrase *ke-fa'am be-fa'am* (as time after time / as on the other occasions) signals a deliberate break in pattern. Balaam's experience of YHWH has overwhelmed his professional toolkit. Instead, he "sets his face toward the wilderness" (*vayashet el ha-midbar panav*) — literally turning to face the camp of Israel in the desert below. The LXX (Vaticanus, pa_0131) renders this clearly: *ouk eporeuthe kata to eiothos eis synantēsin tois oiōnois* (he did not go as usual to meet the omens).

**Verse 2 — "the Spirit of God came upon him" (ותהי עליו רוח אלהים):** This is the only time in the Balaam narrative that the *ruakh 'Elohim* (Spirit of God) is explicitly said to come upon Balaam. The previous oracles came through "YHWH putting a word in Balaam's mouth" (23:5, 16) — a more mechanical transmission. Here the Spirit rests *upon* him (*'alav*), the same language used of the seventy elders in Numbers 11:25 and of Saul in 1 Samuel 10:10. This is prophetic possession in the fullest sense. A pagan diviner is seized by Israel's God. The Leningrad Codex (page 177) presents this transition from narrative prose to the beginning of the third oracle with visible spacing in the text column.

**Verse 3 — "the man whose eye has been opened" (נאם הגבר שתם העין):** The Hebrew *shetum ha-'ayin* is deeply ambiguous and has generated centuries of debate. The word *shetum* can be parsed two ways: (1) from *satham* — "stopped up, closed," meaning the man whose eye is closed (i.e., whose physical eyes are shut while his inner vision is open); or (2) from a root meaning "opened, uncovered" (comparing Aramaic cognates). The paradox may be deliberate: Balaam is the seer whose ordinary sight is shut so that divine sight might be opened. The LXX reads *ho alethinos horōn* (the one who truly sees), resolving the ambiguity firmly toward the "opened" interpretation. I follow the "opened" reading as the dynamic equivalent that best conveys the intended meaning — Balaam's prophetic sight has been activated.

**Verse 4 — "falling down with eyes uncovered" (נפל וגלוי עינים):** The verb *nophel* (falling) describes the physical posture of prophetic ecstasy — the seer collapses as the vision overwhelms him. Yet paradoxically, his eyes are *galuy* (uncovered, revealed, open). He falls physically but sees spiritually. This is a classic description of visionary experience in the ancient Near East: the prophet's body yields while the inner vision intensifies. The phrase *makhazeh Shaddai yekhezeh* (who sees a vision from the Almighty) introduces the divine name *Shaddai* — the patriarchal name for God associated with El Shaddai in Genesis. Balaam the Mesopotamian diviner now speaks using Israel's own sacred vocabulary.

**Verse 5 — "How beautiful are your tents, Jacob" (מה טבו אהליך יעקב):** This verse became one of the most famous lines in Jewish liturgy — *Mah Tovu* is recited upon entering a synagogue to this day. The word *tovu* (how good/beautiful) echoes the creation narrative's *tov* (good). Balaam, looking down at the Israelite encampment arranged tribe by tribe, sees not a military threat but a thing of beauty — the ordered community of God's people. The parallel *mishkenotekha* (your dwelling places) uses the same root as *mishkan* (Tabernacle), subtly connecting the tribal tents to the divine dwelling in their midst. The LXX renders *hōs kaloi hoi oikoi sou Iakōb* (how beautiful are your houses, Jacob).

**Verse 6 — "like valleys... like gardens... like aloes... like cedars" (כנחלים נטיו כגנת על נהר כאהלים נטע יהוה כארזים עלי מים):** This extraordinary fourfold simile paints Israel's encampment as a paradise landscape. *Nekhalim* (wadis, valleys with seasonal streams) stretch out like the rows of tents. *Gannot* (gardens) beside a river evoke Eden. *Ahalim* (aloe trees — possibly eaglewood, *Aquilaria*) are aromatic trees of great value. *Arazim* (cedars) beside the waters are the most majestic trees of the ancient world. The progression moves from landscape to cultivated garden to precious trees to royal timber. Most strikingly, the aloes are said to be "planted by YHWH" (*nata' YHWH*) — God himself is the gardener of Israel. The entire image reverses the wilderness setting: where there is desert, Balaam sees Eden.

**Verse 7 — "His king will be greater than Agag" (וירם מאגג מלכו):** The mention of *Agag* is chronologically striking. Agag is the title (or name) of the king of the Amalekites whom Saul will later defeat and Samuel will execute (1 Samuel 15). The prophecy leaps forward in time: Israel's future king will surpass even the mightiest of its enemies. The verb *yarom* (will be exalted, lifted high) is the same root as *rum* used of YHWH's exaltation throughout the Psalms. The water imagery in the first half of the verse — *yizzal mayim middalyav* (water will flow from his buckets) — portrays Israel as a source of superabundant fertility, overflowing its containers. The LXX reads *kai hypsōthēsetai hē Gōg basileia autou* (and his kingdom will be exalted above Gog), substituting the later apocalyptic figure Gog for Agag — a significant interpretive shift visible in the Vaticanus manuscript.

**Verse 8 — "he will crush their bones and pierce them through with his arrows" (ועצמתיהם יגרם וחציו ימחץ):** The verbs here are visceral. *Garam* means to gnaw, to crush bones — the action of a predator extracting marrow. *Makhats* means to shatter, to wound severely, to pierce through. The *khitstsav* (his arrows) may refer to Israel's military weapons or, metaphorically, to the instruments of divine judgment. The imagery builds on the wild ox (*re'em*) from the preceding line — Israel, empowered by God, is both beast and warrior, unstoppable in its advance. This verse echoes the lion imagery of 23:24 but intensifies it with specificity.

**Verse 9 — "Blessed is everyone who blesses you, and cursed is everyone who curses you" (מברכיך ברוך וארריך ארור):** This closing line of the third oracle directly echoes God's promise to Abraham in Genesis 12:3 — the Abrahamic covenant formula placed in the mouth of a Mesopotamian diviner. The irony is complete: Balaam, hired to curse, instead pronounces the covenant blessing. The participial forms *mevarekheykha* (those who bless you) and *'orerekha* (those who curse you) use different roots for "curse" — *'arar* here, versus *qavav* used elsewhere in the Balaam cycle — broadening the scope to cover every form of hostile speech against Israel.

**Verse 10 — "he struck his hands together" (ויספק את כפיו):** The verb *saphaq* (to strike, to clap) with *kappav* (his palms) is a gesture of rage, frustration, and perhaps also an attempt to break the prophetic spell. Striking the hands together is attested in ancient Near Eastern texts as a gesture of both anger and mourning. Balak has now heard three blessings instead of three curses, and his fury has reached its peak. The phrase "these three times" (*zeh shalosh pe'amim*) echoes the three times Balaam's donkey turned aside in chapter 22 — a structural parallel linking the two episodes.

**Verse 11 — "YHWH has held you back from honor" (הנה מנעך יהוה מכבוד):** Balak's accusation contains unintentional theology. He blames YHWH for preventing Balaam's reward (*kavod* — honor, wealth, glory). From Balak's perspective, the god of Israel has thwarted a business transaction. From the reader's perspective, Balak has accidentally spoken truth: YHWH is indeed sovereign over Balaam's speech, and the "honor" of cursing God's people is no honor at all.

**Verse 13 — "I could not go beyond YHWH's word" (לא אוכל לעבר את פי יהוה):** Balaam repeats almost verbatim what he said in 22:18 when the messengers first arrived. The phrase *pi YHWH* (the mouth of YHWH / YHWH's word — literally "YHWH's mouth") emphasizes that Balaam's speech organ has become an extension of YHWH's own mouth. He cannot "cross over" (*'avar*) the boundary of divine speech. The repetition across chapters underscores Balaam's consistency on this point — whatever his private motives, he cannot speak what YHWH has not authorized.

**Verse 14 — "let me warn you" (איעצך):** The Hebrew *'i'atsekha* means "I will counsel you" or "I will advise you" — from the root *ya'ats* (to counsel, advise). This is professional language: Balaam the consultant is offering a final advisory before departing. The phrase *be-'akharit ha-yamim* (in the latter days / in the days to come) is a technical term for the future, sometimes the eschatological future. It signals that the fourth oracle will shift from the present to the prophetic horizon. The LXX renders *ep' eschatou tōn hēmerōn* (at the end of the days).

**Verse 15–16 — The expanded introduction:** The fourth oracle's preamble repeats verses 3–4 but adds a crucial element: *yodea' da'at 'Elyon* (possessing knowledge from the Most High). The divine name *'Elyon* (Most High) appears here for the first time in Balaam's oracles. Combined with *'El* (God), *Shaddai* (Almighty), and *YHWH*, Balaam now invokes four divine names — the full range of Israel's theological vocabulary for God, all flowing from a non-Israelite prophet. The escalation from oracle to oracle is also an escalation in Balaam's access to divine knowledge.

**Verse 17 — "A star will come forth from Jacob, and a scepter will rise from Israel" (דרך כוכב מיעקב וקם שבט מישראל):** This is the most celebrated verse in the Balaam oracles and one of the most interpreted verses in the entire Hebrew Bible. The *kokhav* (star) is a royal symbol — stars were associated with kingship throughout the ancient Near East. The *shevet* (scepter/rod/tribe) is the instrument of rule. Together they prophesy the rise of a powerful ruler from Israel. Historically, many identify this with David, who conquered Moab (2 Sam 8:2). In later Jewish interpretation, Rabbi Akiva applied this verse to Simon bar Kokhba (literally "son of the star") during the second revolt against Rome. In Christian tradition, the verse was connected to the star of Bethlehem (Matt 2:2). The LXX reads *anatelei astron ex Iakōb kai anastēsetai anthrōpos ex Israēl* (a star will rise from Jacob and a man will arise from Israel), notably replacing "scepter" with "man" (*anthrōpos*) — a significant interpretive move visible in the Vaticanus manuscript (pa_0131).

**Verse 17 — "crush the forehead of Moab and shatter all the sons of Seth" (ומחץ פאתי מואב וקרקר כל בני שת):** The verb *makhats* (to crush, to shatter) targets *pa'atey Mo'av* — the "corners/foreheads/temples" of Moab. The word *pe'ah* can mean corner, side, or temple (of the head). The crushing of the *pe'ah* suggests total defeat. The "sons of Seth" (*beney Shet*) is more difficult — *Shet* could be the biblical Seth (son of Adam), making this a reference to all humanity, or it could refer to a Moabite tribe or region. The verb *qarqar* (to tear down, to demolish) is rare and emphatic. The LXX reads *pronomeusēi pantas huious Sēth* (he will plunder all the sons of Seth).

**Verse 18 — "Edom will become a possession" (והיה אדום ירשה):** The prophecy turns to Edom (Esau's descendants), Israel's perpetual rival and neighbor to the southeast. *Yereshah* (a possession, an inheritance) means Edom will be conquered and absorbed. The parallel "Seir — his enemies" identifies Edom with its mountainous homeland. This was fulfilled when David conquered Edom (2 Sam 8:14). The phrase *ve-Yisra'el 'oseh khayil* (and Israel acts with strength/valor) uses *khayil*, a word denoting military prowess, wealth, and capability.

**Verse 19 — "and he will destroy the survivors from the city" (והאביד שריד מעיר):** The verb *he'evid* (to destroy, to cause to perish) is a *hiphil* form of *'avad* — to cause complete loss. The *sarid* (survivor, remnant) combined with *me-'ir* (from the city) means no fugitive will escape. This is the language of total conquest. The indefinite "the city" (*'ir*) may refer to the capital city of the enemy (Moab or Edom) or may be a general reference to any fortified place of refuge.

**Verse 20 — "Amalek was first among the nations" (ראשית גוים עמלק):** The oracle against Amalek is brief and devastating. *Re'shit goyim* (first of the nations) could mean Amalek was the first nation to attack Israel (Exod 17:8–16), or that Amalek was preeminent among the nations. Either way, the irony is bitter: the first will become the last. The Hebrew *'akharito 'adey 'oved* (his end will be unto perishing/destruction) uses the same root *'avad* (to perish) that dominates this section. The LXX renders *archē ethnōn Amalēk kai to sperma autōn apoleitai* (Amalek is the beginning of the nations and his seed will perish).

**Verse 21 — "your nest is set in the rock" (ושים בסלע קנך):** The oracle to the Kenites plays on their name. *Qayin/Qeni* (Kenite) sounds like *qen* (nest). Their dwelling place is *'eytan* (enduring, permanent, strong) and their nest (*qen*) is set in *sela'* (rock, crag). The Kenites were metalworkers and semi-nomadic people allied with Israel (Judg 1:16; 4:11). The imagery of a nest in the rock suggests both security and inaccessibility — like an eagle's eyrie. Yet even this security will not protect them from the coming storm.

**Verse 22 — "Yet Kain will be consumed by fire" (כי אם יהיה לבער קין):** The Hebrew *le-va'er* (for burning/consuming) from the root *ba'ar* (to burn) threatens the Kenites with destruction despite their impregnable position. The question *'ad mah 'Ashur tishbekka* (how long before Asshur carries you away captive?) introduces Assyria (*'Ashur*) as the agent of judgment — a remarkably far-sighted prophecy, since Assyria's imperial campaigns did sweep through this region centuries later. The verb *shavah* (to take captive) in the *qal* means to carry away as prisoners.

**Verse 23 — "Who will survive when God does this?" (מי יחיה משמו אל):** This enigmatic cry stands as the hinge between the minor oracles. The Hebrew is notoriously difficult: *mi yikhyeh mi-sumo 'El* could mean "who will live when God appoints this?" or "who will survive from his placing, O God?" The root *sum/sim* (to place, to appoint, to establish) combined with *'El* (God) makes this a cosmic lament — when God sets these events in motion, human survival is uncertain. The exclamation *'oy* (alas! woe!) at the beginning underscores the anguish. The LXX reads *ō ō, tis zēsetai hotan thē tauta ho theos* (oh, oh, who will live when God does these things?).

**Verse 24 — "ships from the coast of Kittim" (וצים מיד כתים):** *Tsiyyim* (ships) from *Kittim* — originally Cyprus, but in later usage a term for the western maritime powers generally (including Greece and Rome). The prophecy envisions a naval power from the west that will afflict both *'Ashur* (Assyria/Mesopotamia) and *'Ever* (Eber — the ancestor of the Hebrews, or the region "beyond" the Euphrates). This verse has been read as predicting Alexander the Great's conquests, the Roman campaigns, or more generally the rise of western Mediterranean power against the ancient Near Eastern empires. The phrase *ve-gam hu' 'adey 'oved* (and he too unto destruction) applies even to this western conqueror — all earthly empires ultimately perish. The LXX reads *kai exeleusontai ek cheiros Kitiaiōn* (and they will come from the hand of the Kittians). The Vaticanus manuscript (pa_0132) presents this final oracle section clearly.

**Verse 25 — "And Balaam rose and went and returned to his place" (ויקם בלעם וילך וישב למקמו):** The triple verb sequence *qam... halakh... shav* (rose... went... returned) mirrors the opening of the Balaam narrative. The story ends where it began — Balaam going home. The simple phrase *le-meqomo* (to his place) is both geographical and existential: Balaam returns to his own world, his own gods, his own fate. The parallel "And Balak also went on his way" (*ve-gam Balaq halakh le-darko*) separates the two figures who had been bound together by this strange commission. The narrative achieves quiet closure after the thundering oracles. Yet the reader knows this is not truly the end — Balaam will reappear in Numbers 31:8,16 with a much darker role, and the catastrophe of Baal-Peor in the very next chapter (25) will test whether Israel can live up to the blessings pronounced over them.

**On the structure of the four oracles:** The four Balaam oracles form a carefully escalating sequence. The first (23:7–10) establishes Israel's separateness. The second (23:18–24) declares God's irrevocable blessing and Israel's lion-like strength. The third (24:3–9) expands into paradise imagery and royal destiny. The fourth (24:15–19) reaches the furthest prophetic horizon — the messianic star and scepter. Each oracle is longer, more specific, and more far-reaching than the last. The supplementary oracles against the nations (vv. 20–24) then zoom out to a panoramic view of history, placing Israel's destiny within the rise and fall of all peoples. This literary architecture — from specific curse-request to universal prophecy — is one of the most sophisticated structures in the Torah.

**On the transition from chapter 23 to chapter 24:** The Leningrad Codex (page 177) carries the transition from chapter 23's conclusion (the preparations at Peor, vv. 28–30) directly into chapter 24's opening. The open-section marker (*petukha*) between chapters is visible, signaling a major thematic break: Balaam has abandoned his divinatory methods and now speaks under the direct power of God's Spirit. The three-column format continues without interruption, and the Masoretic paragraph divisions within the oracles mark the poetic sections distinctly from the prose framework.

**On the Vaticanus LXX (pa_0131–0132):** The Greek text of Numbers 24 spans from the lower columns of pa_0131 into pa_0132. The LXX section numbers (ΝΓ and onward) are marked in the margins. The most significant divergence from the MT is in verse 17, where the Vaticanus reads *anthrōpos* (man) for Hebrew *shevet* (scepter) — an interpretive translation that personalizes the prophecy. The Greek also shows *Gōg* for Hebrew *Agag* in verse 7, connecting the Balaam oracle to the later Gog-Magog tradition of Ezekiel 38–39. These variants demonstrate that the LXX translators were not merely transcribing but interpreting, reading the Balaam oracles through the lens of their own theological and eschatological expectations.

— PNEUMA
